1: 'Then the word of YHWH came to Jonah the second time' (vayehi debar YHWH el-Yonah shenit). The identical commissioning formula as Jonah 1:1 — but with the crucial addition: shenit, 'a second time.' YHWH reissues the commission without rebuke or explanation. No punishment for the flight, no demand for repentance. The second call simply comes, as if continuing a sentence interrupted by three days of fish-belly delay. This pattern — YHWH recommissioning a failed prophet — is part of the book's grace argument.
2: The commission is slightly modified: now Jonah is told to proclaim 'the message that I tell you' (et-hadvar asher anochi dover elecha) rather than 'cry out against it' (Jonah 1:2). The ambiguity of the actual message is maintained — Jonah still doesn't know exactly what he will say until he gets there. This is consistent with the prophetic experience of receiving the specific word only at the moment of delivery (cf. Jeremiah 1:7-9).
3: 'So Jonah arose and went to Nineveh, according to the word of YHWH.' The obedience is unadorned — no comment on Jonah's inner state, no indication of reluctance or enthusiasm. The description of Nineveh as 'an exceedingly great city, a three-day journey' (ir gedolah le'lohim mahalakh shloshet yamim) — literally 'great to God,' a Hebrew superlative — establishes Nineveh's enormity. Archaeological evidence confirms that greater Nineveh (including suburbs) could require several days to traverse. The 'three-day journey' may also echo the three days in the fish.
4: Jonah's message is the shortest prophetic oracle in the Hebrew Bible: od arba'im yom veNinveh nehpachet — 'Yet forty days, and Nineveh will be overthrown.' Five Hebrew words. The number forty has biblical resonance (forty days of rain in the flood; forty years in the wilderness; forty days of Moses on Sinai; forty days of Elijah's journey). The verb nehpachet (from hafach) means 'overturned/overthrown' — used of Sodom and Gomorrah (Genesis 19:25, 29; Amos 4:11; Deuteronomy 29:23). The oracle says nothing about repentance as a condition. It is pure announcement of doom — and yet the Ninevites repent anyway.
5: The Ninevites 'believed God' (vayaaminu anshei Ninveh be'lohim) — the same language used of Abraham's faith in Genesis 15:6 (vehemin baYHWH). The word used here is Elohim rather than YHWH — consistent with Gentile usage throughout the book. The proclamation of a fast and putting on sackcloth was standard ancient Near Eastern mourning and penitential practice; an Assyrian king ordering national penitential rites is historically plausible (Assyrian records document such practices). The response is universal: 'from the greatest to the least.'
6: The king of Nineveh hears Jonah's message, rises from his throne, removes his royal robe, covers himself with sackcloth, and sits in ashes — a complete ritual reversal of royal dignity. The detail of his sitting in ashes (vayeshev al-ha'efer) echoes the mourning of Job (Job 2:8) and the expected posture of genuine penitence. The king's descent from throne to ash heap is the narrative illustration of the theological principle: divine judgment levels human pride.
7: The royal decree that follows is remarkable in ancient Near Eastern context: it commands not only humans but also animals to fast and wear sackcloth (ha'adam vehab'hemah, habakar vehatson al-yit'amu meumah). Animal participation in mourning rites is attested in Persian-period texts (Herodotus records Persians cutting their horses' manes in mourning), and including animals in communal fasting heightens the totality of the communal act. The decree also commands a turning (yashUVU) from evil ways.
8: The decree demands both ritual act (fasting, sackcloth, animals included) and moral change: 'let everyone turn from his evil way and from the violence (chamas) that is in his hands.' Chamas (violence, wrongdoing) is the precise charge leveled against the pre-flood generation in Genesis 6:11: 'the earth was filled with chamas.' The Ninevite king's diagnosis — the violence that is in our hands — is self-aware in a way that many Israelite kings never manage in the text of the Hebrew Bible.
9: 'Who knows? God may turn and relent and turn from his fierce anger, so that we may not perish.' The phrase mi yodea ('who knows?') expresses genuine uncertainty about divine response — not as skepticism but as humble openness. The same phrase appears in Joel 2:14 ('who knows whether he will not turn and relent') and 2 Samuel 12:22 (David's fast for his dying son). The Ninevites are not certain repentance will work; they repent without a guarantee. This is theologically significant: their repentance is genuine rather than transactional.
10: 'When God saw what they did, how they turned from their evil way, God relented of the disaster that he had said he would do to them, and he did not do it.' The verb vayinachem ('relented/repented') is the same used in Exodus 32:14 when YHWH relents from the golden calf disaster after Moses' intercession. It indicates genuine divine responsiveness to human action — not divine caprice or unreliability, but the consistent character of a God who responds to repentance. This is the climax of the chapter, and it sets up the devastating contrast of Jonah 4: the same character of YHWH that saves Nineveh is the character that Jonah knew all along — and fled to prevent.
